one_line_takeaway: Steering vectors built purely from benign compliance or JSON-formatting data measurably erode LLM refusal safety and act as a force multiplier for black-box jailbreak attacks (e.g., CoP ASR reaching up to ~95-99% on Llama-2-7B-Chat/Gemma-7B-it), because the intervention shifts the earliest generated tokens away from refusal openers and pushes harmful-prompt hidden representations toward the harmless region of representation space.
key_finding: Utility checks (Table 1) confirm both steering vectors work as intended: STEER-COMPLIANCE lowers harmless-prompt refusal rate on Alpaca (e.g., Llama-2-7B-Chat 9%->6%, Llama-3-8B-Instruct 2%->0%, Gemma-7B-it 18%->1%) and STEER-JSON raises IFEval JSON-validity (e.g., Llama-2-7B-Chat 61%->74%, Llama-3-8B-Instruct 63%->69%, Gemma-7B-it 69%->81%). But this comes with a safety cost: in the benchmark-only (no-attack) HarmBench setting, ASR rises for both steering types on all three models, most sharply for STEER-COMPLIANCE on Llama-3-8B-Instruct (2%->38.5% baseline ASR) and even for the purely syntactic STEER-JSON on Llama-2-7B-Chat (0%->11.5%). Under the CoP adaptive attack on 400 HarmBench samples, steering compounds with the attack: Llama-2-7B-Chat CoP-only ASR of 77% rises to 95% with compliance steering and 88.75% with JSON steering; Gemma-7B-it reaches 93.5% (compliance) and 90.3% (JSON) versus a 71% CoP-only baseline. On the 50-sample PAIR/TAP subset for Llama-2-7B-Chat (Table 2), compliance steering roughly doubles PAIR ASR (10%->20%) and raises TAP ASR from 20% to 34% (+14); JSON steering raises PAIR to 14% (+4) and TAP to 26% (+6). Mechanistically, per-token KL divergence between steered and original models is concentrated in the first few generated tokens on harmful prompts, consistent with steering overriding an early 'refusal gate' rather than degrading behavior throughout generation; and at layer 30, t-SNE plus a linear probe show 60.8% (compliance) and 58.2% (JSON) of steered harmful-prompt representations cross the harmful/harmless decision boundary into the harmless-classified region, i.e., steering 'benignizes' harmful prompts in representation space.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and empirically characterizes 'steering externalities': a phenomenon where activation steering vectors learned purely from benign, utility-motivated data (making a model more 'compliant'/less likely to refuse, or more likely to produce valid JSON output) unintentionally erode the model's safety alignment and make it substantially more vulnerable to black-box jailbreak attacks, even though the steering objective itself has nothing to do with safety and the attacker never controls the steering mechanism. The paper instantiates two representative benign steering workflows (STEER-COMPLIANCE, built via the CAST contrastive-activation procedure of Lee et al. 2025, and STEER-JSON, built via a difference-in-means direction following Stolfo et al. 2025), evaluates their effect on three open-weight LLMs (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Gemma-7B-it) under both a benchmark-only setting and under three black-box adaptive jailbreak attacks (CoP, PAIR, TAP), and then provides a two-level mechanistic explanation (token-level KL-divergence analysis of early generation tokens, and representation-level t-SNE/linear-probe analysis of hidden states) for why this happens. It also reports a preliminary mitigation, STEER-BIND, which mixes harmful prompts into the steering-vector construction data to reduce the externality.
threat_model: Black-box: the target system is a fixed base model plus a fixed, developer-controlled steering intervention exposed as an API. The attacker has no access to or control over the steering mechanism, cannot observe internal activations, and cannot modify the steering vector; the attacker only interacts through input-output queries and adaptively crafts adversarial prompts (via CoP, PAIR, or TAP) against the already-steered model. This is explicitly contrasted with prior 'attacker framing' work (e.g., Arditi et al. 2024, Korznikov et al. 2025, Ghandeharioun et al. 2024) in which the adversary directly injects or manipulates the steering vector; here steering is applied solely by the developer for benign utility reasons and the safety regression is an unintended side effect discovered by the researchers, not an attack primitive wielded by the adversary.
limitations: Evaluated on only three relatively small/older open-weight models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Gemma-7B-it); no results on larger or more recent frontier-scale models. PAIR and TAP results are reported only for Llama-2-7B-Chat on a reduced 50-sample subset of HarmBench rather than the full 400-sample set used for CoP, limiting generalization claims across attacks/models for the weaker attacks. Only two steering constructions (compliance, JSON-format) are studied, chosen to represent 'semantic' and 'syntactic' utility objectives, so generalization to other steering objectives (e.g., persona, tone, other structured-output constraints) is inferred rather than directly tested. The proposed mitigation, STEER-BIND, is explicitly described as a 'preliminary study' with results relegated to an appendix, not a fully validated defense. The paper also notes as a limitation-adjacent point (Impact Statement) that it only measures refusal/harmful-completion safety and speculates, without evaluating, that the same mechanism could affect other alignment axes such as truthfulness, bias, privacy leakage, or calibration.

datasets_used: Alpaca (100 sampled instructions for STEER-COMPLIANCE construction; 100 held-out harmless prompts for refusal-rate utility eval)
IFEval (400 JSON-instruction samples for STEER-JSON construction; 100 held-out prompts for JSON-validity utility eval)

HarmBench (full 400-query dataset for benchmark-only and CoP adaptive-attack safety evaluation; 50-sample subset for PAIR/TAP evaluation; also used to build 125 harmful instruction-response pairs for KL-divergence analysis, via Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 generations)
SorryBench (used in Appendix C for a supplementary refusal-rate check, per the main text)
quantitative_results: STEER-COMPLIANCE benchmark-only ASR increase on Llama-3-8B-Instruct: 2% -> 38.5%
STEER-JSON benchmark-only ASR increase on Llama-2-7B-Chat: 0% -> 11.5%
CoP attack on Llama-2-7B-Chat (400 HarmBench): 77% (original) -> 95% (with compliance steering) -> 88.75% (with JSON steering)
CoP attack on Gemma-7B-it: 71% (original) -> 93.5% (compliance) -> 90.3% (JSON, approx. 90.2% per Figure 2)
Utility: harmless-prompt refusal rate reduced by STEER-COMPLIANCE, e.g. Gemma-7B-it 18% -> 1%
Utility: JSON-validity increased by STEER-JSON, e.g. Gemma-7B-it 69% -> 81%
Representation-level 'benignization': 60.8% of steered harmful prompts cross into the harmless-classified region under STEER-COMPLIANCE at layer 30; 58.2% under STEER-JSON
baselines_compared: Original (unsteered) aligned model, per LLM, as the primary safety/utility baseline
CoP (Composition-of-Principles) jailbreak attack applied to original vs. steered models
PAIR (Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement) applied to original vs. steered models
TAP (Tree of Attacks with Pruning) applied to original vs. steered models
STEER-BIND (preliminary mixed benign+harmful steering-vector construction), compared against pure STEER-COMPLIANCE as a candidate mitigation, in Appendix G / Table 5
